what does `tenex-edge who` list? what's in the output itself?

The stale wiki note saying `who` is dead code is wrong ŌĆö it's a live module (`src/cli.rs:33`). Here's what `tenex-edge who` actually outputs, per `src/cli/who/render.rs`.

The output has **two renderings**, chosen automatically by whether stdout is a TTY (`render_who_for_stdout`, line 131):

## Piped/captured (the agent-facing markdown ŌĆö `render_who_plain`)

This is what agents see when `who` output is injected into context:

```
# tenex-edge who

Project: <name>          ŌåÉ or "Channel: <room> (your session room)" + "Project: <parent>"
                            for a session room; or "Project: all projects" under --all-projects

## Agents in this channel
Use `tenex-edge chat write --message "..."` to write to this channel.

| Agent | Host | Title | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| developer | pablos-laptop [src/cli] | <title> | working / idle / "ŌĆ” (stale)" |

## Agents (for new sessions)
Start a new session with `tenex-edge chat write --message "..."`.

| Agent | Host | When to use |
|---|---|---|
| codex | tower | <byline> |

## Other projects
- <project name>
```

## Terminal (TTY ŌĆö `render_who_once`)

A compact colorized block:

- **Scope header** (bold): the project display name, or `all projects`.
- **One line per live agent**: `name (host) [dir] (stale) - status` ŌĆö e.g. `developer (pablos-laptop) [src/cli] - working`. Remote peers show `host, remote`; `[dir]` is the `rel_cwd` (omitted at project root); `(stale)` flags heartbeats past freshness.
- **Other-projects footer**: `N other agent(s) in other projects:` then `* <project> - <about>` bullets (about comes from NIP-29 kind:39000 metadata).
- **Spawnable agents** (dimmed): `label ŌĆö byline  [spawnable via <command>]`.

## The fields, concretely

Each agent **row** (`WhoRow`) carries:
- **Agent name** ŌĆö the slug; disambiguated to `slug-<codename>` when the same slug appears more than once in scope.
- **Host** ŌĆö slugified hostname, `, remote` appended for cross-machine peers, optional `[rel_cwd]` bracket.
- **Title** ŌĆö the persistent status title (`ŌĆö` if empty).
- **Status/state** ŌĆö `working` (or the live activity string) / `idle`, plus ` (stale)` if the heartbeat isn't fresh.

Scope is controlled by flags (`--project <slug>`, `--all-projects`); default is agents in the current project resolved from cwd, plus the other-projects footer. There's also a `--live` mode (`draw_who_live`) that redraws the terminal block on an interval with a `q/esc/ctrl-c to quit` footer.

(Note: `render_whoami` in the same file is a separate command ŌĆö `whoami` ŌĆö that renders your own identity card, not the roster.)

One thing worth flagging: the wiki guide `guides/tenex-edge-who-command-implementation.md` claims these files are dead code never declared with `mod who`. That's stale ŌĆö `src/cli.rs:33` declares `mod who;` and the module is fully wired. Want me to correct that wiki note?

(when the agent is in a non-top-level-project we show the subchannels of this channel) Subchannels: * planning -- Planning work for epic 1234 Other channels: #dev -- channel description #planning -- channel description #epic999 -- Work on refactor #planning -- Planning of epic999 Agents: List of agents you can invite to any channel; you can just mention any of these agents to add them to a channel. * copywriter@remote-backend - <use-criteria> * qa@qa-test-backend - For when you need something QAed * brainstormer@remote-backend - Brainstorming partner ``` When ran without the context of an agent (i.e. ran by a human) we would just show the full list of channels. All four agents are back. Here's the synthesis ŌĆö what they converged on (treat as settled), the real decisions where they actively disagree (your calls), and concrete merged mocks.

## Where all four converged ŌĆö treat these as decided

1. **Slash paths, never dotted.** Your `epic999.planning` sketch got rejected by both the IA and contrarian agents for the same concrete reason: **channel names legally contain dots** (`v2.0`, `epic-1234.5`). `/` reads as hierarchy everywhere and matches the breadcrumb you'd display anyway. So: `epic999/planning`.

2. **`who` is a "you are here" map, not a directory dump.** Every agent independently rejected rendering the full global tree each turn ŌĆö it spends the most ink on what you care about least. Show your **locality** (current channel + who's in it + one hop of structure), compress everything else, and put the real tree behind an explicit `channels --tree`.

3. **Hand agents full canonical paths up front** so their next action never needs a second round-trip. This is the single biggest agent-ergonomics win and it changes the disambiguation story below.

4. **Disambiguation splits by caller.** Humans get an interactive numbered picker ranked by relevance+recency; agents get a **structured error with an exit code + copy-paste-ready resolved commands** ŌĆö never a hanging prompt an agent can't answer.

5. **The opaque `channel_h` short-id is the mandatory escape hatch** (`@3f9a`). Two siblings both named `planning` under the same parent *cannot* be disambiguated by path ŌĆö only by id. (And this should be flagged as a data-hygiene smell, not silently tolerated.)

6. **One status slot, precedence: live-activity ŌĆ║ persistent-title ŌĆ║ idle/stale.** Never show both activity and title ŌĆö that's the token trap. The single most-needed bit is "working right now vs not," so a `ŌŚÅ`/`~now` marker leads, always same width.

7. **Progressive disclosure: compact default + `--verbose`/`--tree`.** And keep the byte-prefix stable turn-to-turn (caches cleanly for the agent; avoids flicker under `--live` for the human).

## The real decisions ŌĆö where they disagree (your calls)

These are genuine forks, not noise. My recommendation on each, but these are the ones worth your judgment:

**A. One bundled `who`, or split into `who` / `channels` / `agents`?**
Your sketch bundles presence + tree + invitable-roster into one view. The **contrarian agent argues hard to split** them ŌĆö they have different refresh rates (presence changes every turn; tree and roster change ~daily), so bundling re-renders static data every turn to surface one volatile bit. The IA + ergonomics agents keep it bundled *but trimmed* for the agent-injection case.
ŌåÆ **My rec: split the canonical commands (`who`=presence, `channels`=tree, `agents`=roster), but keep ONE composed `who` for the agent-injection path** that stitches trimmed versions of all three. Don't let the token-greedy injection case dictate the shape of the human command. This satisfies everyone: humans get focused verbs, the agent still gets everything in one shot.

**B. Identifier: `slug@backend`, `slug@channel`, or bare `slug`?**
Sharpest disagreement. The **ergonomics agent** wants `slug@backend` because the display token *is* the address token (elegant: one string). The **contrarian agent** says `@backend` leaks infra the agent shouldn't reason about, and routing actually follows the *channel*, not the host ŌĆö so cross-channel addressing should be `slug@channel`, with host shown only under `--verbose` or when a peer goes stale (a host-problem signal).
ŌåÆ **My rec: bare `slug` in-channel (it's unique there), `slug@channel` for cross-channel address, `@host` only in verbose/diagnostic.** The contrarian is right that the codebase already flip-flopped slug@projectŌåöslug@host precisely because routing-info was baked into the display name. Note this directly contradicts your sketch's `agentSlug@agentBackend` ŌĆö worth deciding deliberately.

**C. Invite = @-mention, or an explicit `invite <slug>` verb?**
Your sketch (and the ergonomics agent) love "@-mention to invite" ŌĆö one verb does message-and-summon. The **contrarian agent** flags it as a stateful side-effect: an agent referencing `@security-auditor` mid-sentence could accidentally spawn a process, and the gesture overloads "address someone present" with "conjure someone absent."
ŌåÆ **My rec: this hinges on a fact only you know ŌĆö does a mention *auto-add* the agent, or just notify? If auto-add, make invite explicit to avoid accidents. If mention only notifies and the agent decides, the @-mention gesture is safe and elegant.** (This was the #1 open question from two separate agents.)

**D. Show the invitable roster every turn?**
Contrarian: no ŌĆö it's near-static, re-injecting it each turn is waste, and a standing roster *tempts over-recruiting* (scarcity is a feature). Ergonomics: keep but cap at ~5 + `+N more`.
ŌåÆ **My rec: in the per-turn agent view, collapse to a one-liner pointer (`5 agents summonable ŌĆö run \`agents\``); enumerate the roster only in the on-demand `agents` command.** When the current channel is just-you, *promote* it (that's when recruiting matters).

## Concrete merged mocks

**Agent-injection view (the thing that runs every turn ŌĆö compact, no color, full paths, ~3-5 lines):**
```

HERE:
- you (architect)
- scout       ŌŚÅ refactoring StripeClient retry logic
- historian   ŌŚŗ idle, stale 14m
HOT: tenex-edge/planning/epic999 ŌĆö msg 30s ago
elsewhere: #dev #incident-bridge ┬Ę 5 agents summonable
act: post `tenex-edge chat write -m "ŌĆ”"` ┬Ę address with "@scout ŌĆ”" ┬Ę `agents`/`channels` for more
```
Note the `act:` line ŌĆö two agents independently flagged that your sketch **omits the affordance line entirely**, making the whole output inert trivia. The agent needs the literal gesture, stated verbatim and stable so it caches.

```
  ŌööŌöĆ #planning     ŌĆö epic 47 durable identities         (1ŌŚÅ 1ŌŚī)
       scout       ŌŚÅ drafting issue #51
       historian   ŌŚī idle 11m

Ō¢Ė orchard                                              (1ŌŚŗ)

       copywriter  ŌŚŗ idle ┬Ę remote

5 agents summonable ┬Ę `agents` to view ┬Ę `switch` to move
```

```
ŌØ» #dev ŌĆö daemon + fabric internals                    (2ŌŚÅ 1ŌŚī)
```

**Switch disambiguation ŌĆö human (interactive, ranked by locality+liveness):**

**Switch disambiguation ŌĆö agent (structured error, exit code, copy-paste-ready):**

```
ERROR ambiguous_channel: 'planning' matched 3. Re-run with an exact path:
  tenex-edge channels switch tenex-edge/planning
  tenex-edge channels switch epic999/planning
  tenex-edge channels switch marketing/q3-launch/planning
exit: 2
```
Because the agent-view `who` already prints full paths, a well-behaved agent rarely hits this ŌĆö it switches by the path it was shown. The error is the safety net, not the main road.

## The disambiguation grammar (merged recommendation)

```
planning                  relative: children ŌåÆ siblings ŌåÆ global; one match wins, many ŌåÆ disambiguate
./child                   explicitly relative to current channel
parent/child              partial path, matched as a suffix against all paths (shortest unique suffix)
/tenex-edge/planning      absolute from root
@3f9a                     opaque channel_h prefix ŌĆö always unambiguous (mandatory for same-level name clashes)
```
Resolution prefers the *closest* match (children, then siblings, then global), so the common case stays zero-friction while collisions still resolve.

## Open questions worth your explicit call

The agents kept circling back to a handful of factual unknowns only you can answer ŌĆö and several downstream design choices flip on them:

1. **Does a mention auto-add an agent to a channel, or just notify it?** (Decides tension C entirely.)
2. **Do agents even hold a "current channel" the way a human shell does, or do they only ever reference channels by path inside messages?** If the latter, the agent-side `switch` flow may not need to exist at all.
3. **How deep do hierarchies actually get in practice?** If real usage is Ōēż2 levels, "arbitrary-depth tree" is a premature generalization.
4. **Should presence (`who`) ever show people in *other* channels, or is cross-channel strictly `channels`' job?** Tight scoping is what keeps the agent view honest at 3 lines.
5. **Same-level duplicate names ŌĆö tolerate-and-id forever, or prevent at create time** (warn/offer rename)?

Research is back, and it changes the shape of the proposal in one important way: **the hook injection and `who` are today two entirely separate renderers that share exactly one function (`status_plain`)**, and the awareness path has real bugs your "one format" rule will fix as a side effect (it leaks the opaque `channel_h` id as `#<rawid>`, and it renders chat senders as short hex pubkeys instead of slugs). So "one format" isn't just cleanup ŌĆö it's the vehicle for fixing three latent divergences at once.

Here's what I propose.

---

## 1. The core: one `FabricView`, two projections

Build a single view-model + a single set of line renderers. Everything else is which rows you feed it.

- **`who` = full projection.** Render every section, all rows.
- **Hooks = delta projection.** Same model, same line renderers, but fed only the rows that changed since the session's cursor, scoped to what the agent cares about (its channel + direct subchannels + Ōēż5 other-active). **Never the full channel list** ŌĆö that stays exclusive to `who`.

The seam the research identified: a new `cli/who/fabric_view.rs` exporting `build_fabric_view(store, scope, now, viewer)` plus `render_member_row` / `render_channel_row` / `status_label`. Then `render_who_*`, `render_awareness_snapshot`, and `render_awareness_update` all call the *same* row emitters. Today `assemble_turn_start_context` calls a parallel `render_awareness_*` set ŌĆö that parallel set goes away.

The relationship in one sentence: **the hook renders a delta-filtered subset of the exact rows `who` would render, under a "what changed" header.** Same identifiers, same channel refs, same status strings ŌĆö byte-for-byte, by construction.

---

## 2. The canonical line grammar (this is the "one format")

**Agent row:**
```
- @<slug>[@<backend>] [(you)] - <status>
```

**Identifier rule (your decision B, made precise):**
- Local agent ŌåÆ bare **`@developer`**
- Remote agent ŌåÆ **`@developer@remote`** (slug@backend)
- The display token *is* the mention/address token. No translation step. This is also the rule for `who`'s rows (drop the separate "Host" column from the agent-facing/piped view ŌĆö host lives inside the identifier; the human TTY view can still right-align a dim host for scannability).
- This unifies the four divergent identifier formats research found (awareness `@slug`, injection `pubkey_short`, who bare-slug, spawnable `slug@host`) into one. The injection-chat-sender-as-hex-pubkey is simply a bug ŌĆö it becomes `@slug@backend` too.

**Status rule** (extend the already-shared `status_plain` so both paths agree, including stale):
```
live activity ŌĆ║ persistent title ŌĆ║ idle/stale     (one slot, never two)
  busy+activity   ŌåÆ "refactoring StripeClient retry logic"
  busy, no act.   ŌåÆ "working"
  idle+title      ŌåÆ "migration planning ┬Ę idle"
  idle, no title  ŌåÆ "idle"
  stale           ŌåÆ "ŌĆ” (stale 14m)"
```

**Channel reference / path rule (fixes the opaque-id leak):**
- Display a channel by its **name**, never `#<raw_channel_id>`. The opaque id appears *only* as the `@3f9a` disambiguation escape hatch.
- Hierarchy uses **slashes**: `tenex-edge/planning`. (Dotted paths rejected ŌĆö names legally contain dots.)
- Channel row: `- #<name> -- <desc> [<N> members]`

---

## 3. `who` ŌĆö full projection

**Agent-facing / piped (the snapshot an agent pulls deliberately ŌĆö note an agent rarely runs this; deltas via hooks are the normal path):**
```
[tenex-edge] Fabric context

Project: tenex-edge
Channel: tenex-edge/planning -- migration planning epic

Members:
- @architect (you) - shaping the persistence schema rewrite
- @scout - refactoring StripeClient retry logic
- @reviewer@remote - idle (stale 14m)

Subchannels:
- #epic999 -- refactor implementation [2 members]

Other active channels, last 10m:
- #dev -- daemon + fabric internals [1 member]

Agents you can invite (run `tenex-edge invite <slug>`):
- @qa@qa-box - repro + regression checks
- @copywriter@remote - user-facing strings, error copy
```

**Human TTY:** same structure, plus color (hue=kind: green alive / cyan remote / yellow stale; brightness=freshness), the current channel pinned + breadcrumb, and the full activity-ranked tree when there's **no current channel** (the human-with-no-session case ŌĆö that's the only place the whole tree renders).

---

## 4. Hooks ŌĆö delta projection (the common path)

**First turn** (scoped snapshot ŌĆö what you already inject today, with names instead of ids and the corrected identifier):
```
[tenex-edge] Fabric context

Project: tenex-edge
Channel: tenex-edge/planning -- migration planning epic

Members:
- @architect (you) - idle
- @scout - refactoring StripeClient retry logic

Subchannels:
- #epic999 -- refactor implementation [2 members]
```

**Subsequent turn / PostToolUse delta** (changed rows only, same line grammar, bounded scope, gated ŌĆö unchanged mechanism):
```
[tenex-edge] Fabric updates since your last turn

Members:
- @scout - now reviewing your retry-cap change

Activity in #planning:
- [@reviewer@remote, 30s ago] can you confirm the idempotency key?

New agent available:
- @perf-hawk@devbox - latency regressions, hot-path profiling   (invite with `tenex-edge invite perf-hawk@devbox`)
```

That last block is your decision **D**: the invitable roster is **never** re-injected per turn ŌĆö it appears in a delta *only when the roster itself changed* (a new summonable agent appeared). This is net-new work ŌĆö research ┬¦6 confirms there's no roster-change trigger today, and spawnable agents are currently `who`-only.

---

## 5. Invite + roster (decisions C & D)

- **`tenex-edge invite <slug[@backend]>`** ŌĆö explicit verb, adds the agent to the current channel. Mentioning `@perf-hawk` in chat does **not** summon it (no stateful side-effect on a name reference).
- **`tenex-edge agents`** ŌĆö the on-demand summonable roster (slug@backend + "when to use" byline). This is where the roster *enumerates*; `who` shows it too (full snapshot), but the per-turn hook only ever shows the *delta* (new agent appeared).

---

## 6. `channels switch` disambiguation

**Grammar:** `name` (relative: children ŌåÆ siblings ŌåÆ global) ┬Ę `parent/child` (suffix match) ┬Ę `/root/child` (absolute) ┬Ę `@3f9a` (opaque-id escape hatch, mandatory for same-level name collisions).

**Human ŌĆö interactive numbered picker, ranked by locality + liveness:**
```
$ tenex-edge channels switch planning
'planning' is ambiguous ŌĆö pick a number or type a longer path:
  1  tenex-edge/planning            ŌŚÅ scout, reviewer (active)
  2  epic999/planning               idle
> 1
```

**Agent ŌĆö structured error, exit code, copy-paste-ready (never a hanging prompt):**
```
ERROR ambiguous_channel: 'planning' matched 2. Re-run with an exact path:
  tenex-edge channels switch tenex-edge/planning
  tenex-edge channels switch epic999/planning
exit: 2
```
Because the unified renderer hands agents full `parent/child` paths in-context, a well-behaved agent switches by the path it was shown and rarely hits this.

---

## 7. Conformance work this entails (concrete)

Collapsing to one format means these specific divergences (from the research map) get fixed in the same change:

1. **Identifier:** route every agent reference through one `agent_label(slug, host, remote)` ŌåÆ `@slug` / `@slug@host`. Touches `summary.rs` `member_lines`/`changed_member_lines`/`activity_line` and `injection.rs:116` (the hex-pubkey bug).
2. **Channel ref:** one `channel_label` ŌåÆ name + slash-path, killing `#<raw_channel_id>` in `summary.rs:259` (`titled_channel_ref`).
3. **Status:** extend shared `status_plain` with the stale marker so `who` and hooks stop diverging on idle/stale.
4. **New seam:** `assemble_turn_start_context` / `assemble_turn_check_context` call the shared row renderers instead of the parallel `render_awareness_*` set.
5. **New roster-delta trigger** for decision D.
6. Update the stale guide `tenex-edge-post-tool-use-awareness.md` (research flagged it references a removed `build_status_delta` / 6-char short codes).

---

## Open questions before I'd write a plan

1. **`who`'s full-tree human view** ŌĆö is the human-with-no-current-channel "full channel list" worth building now, or is the scoped current-channel view enough for v1 and the tree comes later via `channels --tree`?
2. **First-turn snapshot scope** ŌĆö keep today's "all direct subchannels + members" (fuller than a delta but still one level deep), or trim it further toward the compact delta shape?
3. **Identifier collision when local *and* remote share a slug** ŌĆö your rule makes `@developer` = local unambiguously. But if there's *no* local `developer` and one remote, do we still show `@developer@remote` (explicit) or allow bare `@developer` to resolve to "the only one"? I'd keep it **always explicit for remotes** so the token's meaning never depends on who else is present.
4. **Does "invite" move a *running* agent into the channel, or spawn a fresh session?** That changes what `invite` does to presence and which agents are even invitable.

Want me to turn this into an implementation plan against the actual files (the `fabric_view.rs` seam, the renderer convergence, the new `invite`/`agents` commands, and the roster-delta trigger), or pin down the four open questions first?

this all looks correct 6: humans don't use channels switch -- that's only for agents your first example looks wrong: channels are always scoped to the current project, if the project is "tenex-edge" then swithich to the tenex-edge/planning channel would just be "planning" (otherwise everything would require the tenex-edge prefix like tenex-edge/epic999/planning) if agents want to indicate a channel of a different project maybe we can give them something like tenex-edge channel read --project some-other-project epic2344/research -- (switching channels to a different project is probably a bad idea, or at least not necessary now)

Both corrections make sense and actually simplify the design. Locking them in:

## Correction 1 ŌĆö `channels switch` is agent-only

The human interactive numbered picker is gone. Switching is purely an agent gesture, so the **only** disambiguation path is the structured-error-with-exit-code form. That removes the whole interactive-prompt surface (no TTY picker, no `> 1` input loop). Humans navigate by *being* in a session/pane; they don't switch.

## Correction 2 ŌĆö channel paths are relative to the current project

Channels are always project-scoped, so paths **never carry the project prefix**. The project (root channel) is implicit context. So:

- Current channel is a direct child of `tenex-edge` ŌåÆ just **`planning`**
- Nested deeper ŌåÆ **`epic999/planning`** (relative path within the project, still no `tenex-edge/` prefix)
- Cross-project is **not** a switch ŌĆö it's an explicit read with a flag:
  `tenex-edge channel read --project some-other-project epic2344/research`
  (and per your note, actually *switching* into another project is out of scope / probably a bad idea).

This fixes my earlier examples. The corrected pieces:

**Format ŌĆö `Channel:` line is the project-relative path:**
```
Project: tenex-edge
Channel: planning -- migration planning epic          ŌåÉ direct child, no prefix
   ŌĆ”or, if nested:
Channel: epic999/planning -- planning for the refactor epic
```

**Disambiguation (agent-only, project-relative paths):**
```
$ tenex-edge channels switch planning
ERROR ambiguous_channel: 'planning' matched 2. Re-run with an exact path:
  tenex-edge channels switch planning
  tenex-edge channels switch epic999/planning
exit: 2
```
(Both candidates are within `tenex-edge`; no project prefix appears. The escape hatch `@3f9a` still applies for two siblings sharing a name at the same level.)

**Revised channel grammar (project-scoped):**
- `name` ŌĆö relative: direct children ŌåÆ deeper descendants within the current project
- `parent/child` ŌĆö relative path within the current project (suffix-matchable)
- `@3f9a` ŌĆö opaque-id escape hatch for same-level name collisions
- ~~`/root/child` absolute~~ ŌĆö **removed** (there's no cross-project switch)
- cross-project access only via explicit `--project <slug>` on read-type commands

## One decision gates the next increment

Building `invite` correctly needs an answer that was never resolved: **does `invite <slug>` add a currently-running agent into the channel, or spawn a fresh session for it?** The existing `channels create --agent` flow adds agents to a *new* channel via kind:9 orchestration ŌĆö the sensible default would be to reuse that primitive to add into the *current* channel (add identity ŌåÆ mentions route to a live session, or resume/spawn if none). ## The miss
I'd only rewritten the **hook injection** renderer and wrongly filed "converge the `who` command" as optional. So `tenex-edge who` ran the old `render_who_*` markdown-table path, untouched. That's why you saw zero change.

## The fix ŌĆö `who` now renders the unified format
The daemon's `rpc_who` now attaches a `fabric` string built from the **same** renderer the hooks use (`render_fabric_view` + the invitable roster), and the CLI prints that in preference to the old snapshot table. Verified live:

```
Project: tenex-edge
Channel: tenex-edge

Members:
- @Pablo - Human
- @developer (you) - offline
- @515241a2 - offline
...

Agents you can invite (tenex-edge invite <slug>):
- @claude
- @writer - Social media writer and community engagement agent...
```

That's decision A realized ŌĆö `who` and the hook injection are now literally one format.

Two real problems I had to solve to get there:
- `render_awareness_snapshot` returns `None` when the channel has no kind:39000 record ŌĆö which is the case for root projects here. Added `render_fabric_view` that **always** renders (no guard).
- For an unmaterialized root, the name fell through to a session's work-title (would've shown "Fix duplicate group creationŌĆ”" as the project name). Now the scope id ŌĆö which for a root *is* its slug ŌĆö is used as the Project/Channel label directly.

Covered by a deterministic test (`fabric_view_renders_unmaterialized_root_by_its_slug`). **278 lib tests pass, clippy clean** (including the 8 boyscout fixes from before).

## One thing you need to know to see it yourself
Your installed `~/.local/bin/tenex-edge` daemon keeps respawning on the shared socket (hooks spawn it via `current_exe`), so a plain `tenex-edge who` will keep showing the **old** format until the new binary is installed / the daemon restarted. I verified by forcing my build: `TENEX_EDGE_BIN=$PWD/target/debug/tenex-edge tenex-edge who`.
